#### MATHEMATICS | SOCIETY | PSYCHOLOGY ### Prologue: The Whisper That Shaped a Species _Homo sapiens_ did not conquer the planet because _individual humans_ acted faster than other animals but because _human groups_ could coordinate better, more flexibly, and at a greater scale. Such complex coordination, so characteristic of humans, depends on a dynamic flow of social understanding. It concerns how alliances are formed, who betrays trust, who acts generously or cruelly, and who proves competent or incompetent. At the latest by the evolutionary caesura of the _Cognitive Revolution_, roughly 70,000 years ago, and against the background of language capacities that may have been present by about 100,000 to 135,000 years ago, this social understanding began to take the form of stories, reputations, and expectations moving from human to human and from mind to mind. Across millennia of human social and cultural development, this communicative practice expanded and grew more complex. It moved from the intimate economies of small bands, where it tracked reputations, punished defectors, and rewarded cooperators, to salons, newspapers, radio, platforms, and now machine-mediated global channels, where even artificial intelligence agents exchange second-hand claims about other agents, third parties, sources, and reputations. At the center of the long history of this communicative practice stands _gossip_, a form of speech about absent others that often carries a poor reputation in moral discourse and polite society but, from an anthropological point of view, belongs among the most evolutionarily consequential forms of human collective communication. It persists with extraordinary ease across cultures and historical periods because it answers a recurrent social need. One reason for this remarkable persistence may lie in how frequently humans gather and exchange information about absent others—who did what, who will do what, who can be trusted, and who cannot. The British biological anthropologist and evolutionary psychologist R. I. M. Dunbar argues that roughly two-thirds of human conversation concerns social topics that fall broadly under the category of _gossip_. [⇱](https://allegatifac.unipv.it/ziorufus/Dunbar%20gossip.pdf) Frequency may explain why gossip is so visible in ordinary conversation, but its _durability_ becomes clearer in Dunbar's comparison with _primate social grooming_. [⇱](https://allegatifac.unipv.it/ziorufus/Dunbar) In primate groups, grooming maintains social bonds through physical touch, whereas language allows humans to _maintain_ and _extend social cohesion_ through reputational information that can circulate far beyond direct bodily contact. Gossip is essential to this transition because it strengthens alliances, helps enforce norms at low cost, and makes violations socially visible beyond one-to-one interaction. The anthropological argument has a simple mathematical implication. If group size grows while individual time remains fixed, _social cohesion becomes a problem of communicative capacity._ It is possible to formalize Dunbar's scaling claim as a simple capacity inequality. Let **_N_** denote group size, and let each individual have a fixed social-maintenance time budget **_τ_** per day. Suppose that maintaining one relationship through _grooming_ is _dyadic_ and costs **_c_****₁** units of time per partner, so one person can actively maintain at most **_d_****₁=****_τ_****/****_c_****₁** relationships per day, up to rounding. By contrast, suppose that a _gossip_ or _talk_ episode costs **_c_****₂** units of time but reaches, on average, **_s_****(****_N_****)** distinct partners per episode, so the effective capacity becomes **_d_****₂=(****_τ_****/****_c_****₂)****_·s_****(****_N_****)**. If cohesion in a group of size **_N_** requires each person to keep at least a fixed fraction **_ρ_** of possible ties "_warm_," meaning **_d≥ρ·_****(****_N−1_****)**, then grooming yields a hard size ceiling **_τ_****/****_c_****₁≥****_ρ_****·(****_N_****−1)**, or equivalently, **_N_****≤1+****_τ_****/(****_ρ_****·****_c_****₁)**. The simulation below compares two update rules on the same fine-meshed hexagonal contact grid. In the dyadic grooming regime, each active cell can update only one neighboring tie per time unit, representing one-to-one social maintenance under a fixed time budget. In the gossip-driven talk regime, each active cell can update several neighboring ties per time unit, representing the amplification factor **_s_****(****_N_****)**. _Yellow cells_ mark positions newly warmed at the current time step, while _green cells_ mark positions that have already become socially warm. The grid, however, is a schematic representation of capacity propagation rather than a literal graph of dyadic ties. The model deliberately keeps contact local in both panels. The only difference lies in the number of ties that can be updated per unit time. This isolates the scaling point. Gossip-driven talk changes the capacity arithmetic of social cohesion. Gossip-driven talk changes the scaling regime because a single episode can reach more than one partner. It shifts social maintenance from a strictly _one-to-one_ mechanism with bounded capacity under fixed **_τ_** to a _one-to-many_ mechanism whose effective capacity can increase through the amplification factor **_s_****(****_N_****)**. The capacity model shows why this is important. Beyond its possible moral or psychological satisfactions, gossip addresses a deeper scaling problem because _group size can grow while each person's daily social-maintenance time remains bounded._ Under that condition, any communicative mechanism that reaches several partners within the same amount of time gains a structural advantage. Dunbar's "grooming to gossip" thesis can therefore be understood as a _scaling transition_ because language, once it supplements touch, changes how many social ties can be maintained under the same time constraint. [⇱](https://museuvirtualstevejobs.weebly.com/uploads/4/7/7/2/47722555/grooming_gossip_and_the_evolution_of_language.pdf) > ***In short, gossip changes the arithmetic of social cohesion.*** A second perspective concerns what gossip does within the minds of individuals and within the moral memory of the group. Gossip may have a modest stabilizing effect at the psychological level, not because it simply reduces stress, but because it can clarify norms, convert uncertainty into shared interpretation, and strengthen an individual's sense of social orientation and group belonging. [⇱](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-023-30126-9) This psychological and cultural layer helps explain why gossip is more than the circulation of loose information. It can function as a form of cultural learning because it encodes norms in narratives about concrete people and concrete consequences. _Baumeister_, _Zhang_, and _Vohs_ argue that gossip teaches people how to live within a culture by relaying norm violations and their social penalties in story form. [⇱](https://assets.csom.umn.edu/assets/71514.pdf) _Foster's_ taxonomy review broadens this view by organizing gossip across social, evolutionary, and personal functions, which helps explain why a behavior with a morally "_bad_" label can still produce value at the level of the group. [⇱](https://smg.media.mit.edu/library/Foster.gossipreview.pdf) The remaining question is why a behavior that may contribute to group cohesion so often receives a negative moral reputation. _Hartung_ and colleagues add a modern empirical layer, showing that gossip serves motives ranging from information exchange and bonding to self-enhancement, while its moral reputation often diverges from its typical social role. [⇱](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6549470/) This essay does not claim to invent a new mathematics of gossip from nothing. It offers a mathematical reading of gossip by bringing together tools that already exist in rumor modeling, network theory, information theory, game theory, point processes, distributed computing, and recommender-system analysis. Whenever a claim about an absent third party moves from one agent to another, it can be treated as a formal object with content, carriers, transmission paths, credibility, incentives, distortion, and reach. The same elements recur in face-to-face conversation, mass media, social platforms, distributed computer systems, and modern AI systems, although each medium changes the speed, cost, topology, and failure modes of transmission. ### 1. A Rumor on a Network and the Classical Daley–Kendall Model A mathematical reading of gossip begins by reducing the phenomenon to its minimal formal structure. Let **_G=_****(****_V,E_****)** represent a social network. A _vertex_ **_i_****∈****_V_** represents a human agent, and an _edge_ **(****_i_****,****_j_****)∈****_E_** represents a communication channel through which a message can pass from _i_ to _j_. A rumor then has two separable components. It has _content_, represented by a _message_ **_m∈_**𝓜, and it has a _state_, represented by each _agent's relation to that message_. In reality, an agent may be unaware, aware but inactive, aware and active, skeptical, committed, or already tired of repeating it. The simplest models, however, suppress the internal structure of **_m_** and treat the rumor as an _index_ whose spread can be tracked. Later models can allow the content to mutate, measure semantic drift, or even couple transmission to incentives and algorithmic curation. Given an initial set of informed agents **_A_****₀⊆****_V_**, _what law governs the set __**Aₜ⊆V**__ of agents aware of the rumor at time __**t**__, and what determines the final reach __**A***__?_ Foundational rumor models answer this question by treating rumor spread as an epidemic-like process. In 1964, _Daley_ and _Kendall_ gave this analogy one of its foundational mathematical forms, now known as the _Daley–Kendall (DK) rumor model_. [⇱](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/14243408/) Their rumor model and its later variants partition a population into three compartments. _Ignorants_ **_I(t)_** have not heard the rumor. _Spreaders_ **_S(t)_** know it and actively transmit it. _Stiflers_ **_R(t)_** know it but no longer spread it. Let **_N_****=|****_V_****|** with **_I_****(****_t_****)+****_S_****(****_t_****)+****_R_****(****_t)=N_**_._ To describe the basic mechanism in a simple directed-contact mean-field form, define: with **_i_****(****_t_****)+****_s_****(****_t_****)+****_r_****(****_t_****)=1**. The dynamics are: Here **𝛽>0** is the _rate at which spreaders contact ignorants and transmit the rumor_, while **𝛼>0** is the _rate at which spreaders become stiflers after contact with already informed agents_, whether spreaders or stiflers. Since **_i_**, **_s_**, and **_r_** are population fractions, both **𝛼** and **𝛽** have units of inverse time. _This stifling mechanism differs significantly from the epidemic picture_. In a disease model, contact with an immune individual may block transmission without changing the infected person's state. In the DK rumor model, contact with an already informed listener can change the teller's state from _spreader_ to _stifler_. Through the quadratic term **–𝛼****_s_****(****_s_****+****_r_****)**, the model represents, in compact form, the loss of novelty and social value as the rumor saturates the population. As the informed population grows, spreaders increasingly meet people who already know the rumor, and those encounters reduce the number of active tellers. In the DK rumor model, the process can therefore burn out before the rumor reaches everyone. _Maki and Thompson's_ 1973 monograph later placed this family of rumor models within a broader mathematical modeling framework and became a canonical reference for the early systematic treatment of rumor dynamics. [⇱](https://archive.org/details/mathematicalmode00maki) The simulation above compares two contact-driven spreading models. The _left_ column shows a mean-field rumor model in the _Daley–Kendall tradition_, while the _right_ column shows a simple SIR epidemic model. The _upper_ panels plot the compartment equations over time, while the lower panels show spatial lattice versions. In both panels, _white_ denotes agents who have not yet been reached, _red_ denotes the active transmitting class, and _blue_ denotes agents who have already left that class. In the rumor model these classes are _ignorants_, _spreaders_, and _stiflers_; in the SIR model they correspond to _susceptible_, _infected_, and _recovered_ agents. The key difference lies in _how the active class disappears_. In the SIR model, infected agents leave the active class through recovery or removal, so the red region expands and then turns blue from within. In the _rumor model_, spreaders become stiflers when they encounter agents who already know the rumor, so the blue region marks saturation of prior knowledge rather than biological recovery. The rumor panel therefore develops a blue interior with a thinner red frontier, and some white cells may remain unreached when the spreaders disappear. The simulation makes visible the role of the stifling term **–𝛼****_s_****(****_s_****+****_r_****)**. As the informed population grows, contacts with already informed agents increasingly suppress further transmission, so a rumor can burn out before reaching everyone. The Daley–Kendall family gives three durable lessons. _First_, rumor spread is self-limiting even without recovery in the epidemiological sense because saturation increases stifling encounters. _Second_, its final reach usually remains below the whole population under standard assumptions because spreaders may stop before all ignorants have been contacted. _Third_, parameters that raise transmission to ignorants, such as a high 𝛽, or reduce stifling, such as a low 𝛼, increase reach, although network topology can become decisive once the mean-field assumption fails. Modern work revisits this paradigm under more realistic assumptions, including _heterogeneous activity, explicit network structure, credibility, hesitation_, and _belief_. _Chen and Wang_, for example, extend the classical compartment approach by introducing rumor credibility and life-correlation parameters, subdividing ignorants by personality type, adding an exposed or hesitating class, and deriving spreading thresholds through next-generation-matrix analysis on homogeneous and heterogeneous networks. [⇱](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-020-62585-9) ### 2. **Network Structure from Small Worlds to Heterogeneous Mean-Field Theory** Mean-field models, such as the Daley–Kendall rumor model, coarse-grain the explicit contact network into a homogeneous-mixing approximation in which interaction rates depend on population averages rather than on individual connections. This is useful as a first approximation, but it discards the graph-topological information that determines the paths through which gossip travels. Real social networks are not smooth populations. Some ties form dense local clusters, some bridge distant groups, some individuals have far more connections than others, and some groups form internally dense regions with fewer external links. These properties are known respectively as _clustering_, _bridging_, _degree heterogeneity_, and _community structure_. Once these features matter, _the same number of spreaders can produce different outcomes depending on their positions in the graph_. A rumor inside a tight cluster may circulate without escaping, while the same rumor near a bridge or a high-degree node may cross into distant parts of the network. Watts and Strogatz, for example, showed that if a small fraction of edges in a sparse network act as long-range shortcuts, the network can retain high local clustering while acquiring short characteristic path lengths. [⇱](https://www.nature.com/articles/30918) This is the _small-world range_, in which local neighborhoods remain dense while distant regions become reachable in relatively few steps. [⇱](https://www.nature.com/articles/30918) > *"Ordinarily, the connection topology is assumed to be either completely regular or completely random. But many biological, technological and social networks lie somewhere between these two extremes." [⇱](https://www.nature.com/articles/30918)* The simulation below explains the Watts–Strogatz rewiring construction. It begins with a regular ring lattice of _n_=60 vertices, where each vertex is connected to its _k_=6 nearest neighbors. As the rewiring probability _p_ increases, long-range shortcuts replace some local edges. _Blue edges_ mark local ties that preserve the ring-lattice neighborhood structure, while _orange edges_ mark rewired shortcut ties. The right panel tracks the normalized characteristic path length **_L_****(****_p_****)/****_L_****(****_0_****)** and the normalized clustering coefficient **_C_****(****_p_****)/****_C_****(0)**. The key point is that these two quantities respond differently to small changes in **_p_**. The characteristic path length **_L_**(**_p_**) falls sharply once a few long-range shortcuts appear because distant parts of the graph become reachable in fewer steps. The clustering coefficient **_C_**(**_p_**), however, remains comparatively high over the same range because most local neighborhoods are still intact. The network remains locally clustered, but it no longer behaves like a large regular lattice at a global scale. For rumor propagation, the consequence is clear. Clustering keeps gossip circulating within local circles, while shortcut ties reduce the number of steps needed to reach distant parts of the network. _Degree heterogeneity_ adds a second structural correction. In a homogeneous mean-field model, every individual is treated as if they had roughly the same number of contact opportunities. In a heterogeneous network, however, some nodes have many more edges than others. These high-degree nodes therefore have more opportunities to receive and transmit a rumor, so transmission can concentrate around them and proceed more rapidly. _Moreno_, _Nekovee_, and _Pacheco_ formalize this correction for rumor spreading on complex networks by deriving mean-field equations conditioned on degree class and comparing homogeneous networks with random scale-free networks. [⇱](https://link.aps.org/doi/10.1103/PhysRevE.69.066130) Let **_iₖ_****(****_t_****)**, **_sₖ_****(****_t_****)**, and **_rₖ_****(****_t_****)** denote the fractions of degree-_k_ nodes in the ignorant, spreader, and stifler compartments. For an uncorrelated network, let **_P_****(****_k_****)** denote the degree distribution and let **⟨****_k_****⟩** denote the mean degree. Define: as the probability that a randomly followed edge leads to a spreader. The exposure term for a degree-_k_ node then scales with **_k_****𝜃(****_t_****)**, rather than with a single global spreader fraction. This change is small in notation but large in meaning. A node with degree **_k_** has **_k_** possible contact channels, so its exposure rate depends on its degree. High-degree nodes therefore tend to learn the rumor efficiently, and when a rumor starts from a high-degree seed, it can approach its eventual level of spread more rapidly. In the simulations reported by Moreno, Nekovee, and Pacheco, the final density of stiflers is essentially independent of the initial seed's degree, while the time needed to reach that value decreases as the degree of the seed increases. The simulation below compares two rumor processes on the same heterogeneous scale-free network under the same update rule. The only difference is the initial seed. In _the_ _left panel_, the rumor begins at a low-degree node, while in _the_ _right panel_ it begins at a hub. Node size indicates degree, so larger nodes have more contact channels. A _black ring_ marks the initial seed in each panel. _White nodes_ are still ignorant, _red nodes_ are active spreaders, _blue nodes_ are stiflers who have learned the rumor but no longer transmit it, and _yellow nodes_ mark agents newly reached at the current time step. The lower-right plot tracks the reached fraction over time under the two initial conditions. The simulation makes the degree-conditioned exposure term **_k_****𝜃(****_t_****)** visible. Starting from a hub accelerates the process because the rumor enters the network through many contact channels at once, allowing the hub-seeded process to achieve broad coverage much earlier than the low-degree-seeded process. This supports the result of _Moreno_, _Nekovee_, and _Pacheco_ that, in heterogeneous networks, the degree of the initial spreader can strongly affect how quickly a rumor approaches its eventual level of spread. Degree heterogeneity explains why some starting points accelerate rumor spread, but it does not by itself answer _when local transmission remains confined_ and _when it develops into a network-wide cascade_. Percolation theory gives one mathematical answer to this question. _Newman_ showed that, for suitable SIR-like processes on networks, final outbreak size can be mapped to bond percolation through a transmissibility parameter **_T_**. [⇱](https://link.aps.org/doi/10.1103/PhysRevE.66.016128) His key move is to replace the temporal spreading process with a static connectivity problem. Suppose each edge has probability **_T_** of successfully carrying transmission during the active period. We keep that edge with probability **_T_** and delete it otherwise. The remaining graph is the percolated graph. The final outbreak from a seed is the set of nodes connected to that seed in this reduced graph. If the reduced graph contains a giant connected component, a single local seed can reach a macroscopic fraction of the network. For gossip, the same reasoning can be applied with care. A gossip transmissibility does not measure infection but the effective probability that a contact occurs, the rumor is passed on, the recipient pays attention, and the recipient becomes a spreader rather than an immediate stifler. Under suitable independence assumptions, the final informed set can then be approximated by a percolation cluster. In this sense, a rumor becomes _viral_ when the effective transmission process crosses the threshold at which the reachable component becomes large. The simulation below shows _a clustered communication network with four local communities connected by a smaller number of bridging ties_. Each edge is treated as a possible channel for gossip transmission and is kept with an effective gossip transmissibility **_T_**__g_. In this percolation picture, a kept edge is an open channel through which the rumor could pass, while a deleted edge is unavailable for transmission. _Pale edges_ are closed, _dark blue edges_ are open, and _red edges_ are open paths that belong to the component reachable from the seed. _Pale nodes_ are unreached, _red nodes_ are reachable from the seed through open edges, and the seed itself is marked in _yellow_. The right panel plots two quantities as **_T_**__g_ increases. The _red curve_ shows the fraction of nodes reachable from the seed, while the _dashed blue curve_ shows the size of the largest open component in the percolated graph. The simulation visualizes why _virality_ is a threshold feature. For small **_T_**__g_, open edges form isolated local islands, so the seed can reach only a small neighborhood. Near the threshold, separate components begin to merge, and a modest increase in **_T_**__g_ can suddenly expand the reachable set. Above the threshold, the seed connects to a large component, so a local rumor can reach a macroscopic fraction of the network. For gossip, **_T_**__g_ should be understood as an effective probability that includes contact, attention, retelling, and the chance that the recipient becomes a spreader rather than an immediate stifler. Percolation theory explains when a rumor can become reachable across a large part of the graph, but _reachability_ is not yet the same as _endorsement_ or _repetition_. Some rumors spread after a single exposure, while others require social confirmation before an agent believes them strongly enough to pass them on. _Granovetter's threshold model_ formalizes this second regime at the aggregate level. In his original formulation, each actor has a threshold, meaning _the number or proportion of others who must already have made a decision before that actor does so._ For rumors, he notes explicitly that people vary in credulity and that _some may need to hear a rumor from more than one person before they believe it strongly enough to spread it._ In the aggregate model, if **_a_****(****_t_****)** denotes the proportion of actors who have already adopted and **_F_** is the cumulative distribution of thresholds, the update rule is **_a_****(****_t_****+1)=****_F_****[****_a_****(t)]**. This is a powerful equation because it shows how collective outcomes can depend sensitively on the exact distribution of thresholds rather than merely on average preferences. Granovetter's own examples show that two populations with nearly identical threshold distributions can generate radically different outcomes when a small gap appears in the sequence of thresholds. A _network-local version_ of this model changes the information available to each actor. Instead of responding to the global participation fraction **_a_****(****_t_****)**, agent **_i_** responds only to the adopted fraction within its own neighborhood **𝒩(****_i_****)**: Here **1{∙}** is the indicator function. The term **_bᵢ_****(****_t_****)∈{0,1}** records whether agent **_i_** has adopted by time **_t_**, and **𝜃****_ᵢ_****∈[0,1]** is agent **_i_**'s adoption threshold. This equation should be read as a graph-theoretic adaptation rather than as Granovetter's original model. It brings into view the social-structural and spatial complications that Granovetter himself identified. These may include friendship weights, local influence, incomplete connectedness, and the possibility that different clusters drawn from similar underlying threshold distributions can produce different local outcomes. The simulation below contrasts Granovetter's original aggregate threshold logic with a network-local adaptation of the same idea. In the _upper panel_, the hexagonal field serves only as a visual representation of the population. Spatial position does not determine adoption. Each cell represents an agent with an individual threshold **𝜃****_ᵢ_**, and once the global participation fraction **_a_****(****_t_****)** crosses that threshold, the agent becomes eligible to adopt regardless of where it lies in the field. This is why adoption appears as _scattered clusters_ across the population. The _pale yellow cells_ mark agents that are already eligible under the current value of **_a_****(****_t_****)**, but whose activation is staggered only to make the animation readable. In Granovetter's exact aggregate recurrence, all eligible agents enter at the next update step. In the _lower panel_, the hexagonal field is treated as a local contact network. Here an agent does not respond to the global fraction **_a_****(****_t_****)**, but only to the adopted fraction among its own neighbors. A cell adopts when that local fraction meets or exceeds its threshold, so adoption advances outward from the initial seed as a spatial frontier. The two panels therefore distinguish Granovetter's aggregate bandwagon effect, described by **_a_****(****_t_****+1)=****_F_****[****_a_****(t)]**, from a graph-theoretic local-threshold cascade, described by the equation above for **_bᵢ_****(****_t_****+1)**. The upper model shows how the distribution of thresholds alone can generate sudden collective adoption. The lower model shows what changes when the same threshold idea is placed on an explicit local contact structure. Adoption no longer depends on the global fraction of adopters, but on neighborhood-level confirmation. In this simplified visualization, the only structural constraints are hex-grid adjacency and local thresholding. More realistic social networks would require additional features such as community structure, bridges, hubs, weighted ties, or bottlenecks. ### 3. Information Theory and Gossip as Noisy Transmission and Semantic Drift The previous models track who has been reached by a rumor, but they do not yet track what happens to the rumor itself. This matters because gossip is almost never repeated without alteration. A story may preserve part of its central claim while losing context, gaining emphasis, acquiring moral color, or becoming more certain than the evidence allows. Let **_X_** represent the underlying state of the world to which the rumor refers, such as whether an event actually occurred. Let **_M_****₀** represent the initial utterance, and let **_M_****₁, ****_M_****₂, …** represent successive retellings. A minimal model treats these retellings as a _Markov chain_ on _messages_: Here the stochastic kernel **_K_** represents the ways a message can change through memory limits, selection, bias, embellishment, omission, and loss. This formulation separates transmission from truth. The message **_Mₜ_** may still refer to **_X_**, but each retelling passes through a channel that can preserve, distort, or discard information. In the idealized Markov setting: the data-processing inequality gives: This inequality says that _a retelling cannot, by itself, increase the mutual information between the message and the underlying truth_. It may inflate the claim, sharpen its emphasis, or increase apparent confidence, but those changes do not count as reliable information unless an external source enters the chain. Repeated circulation can also stabilize a claim socially, turning it into a pseudo-fact that a group treats as shared knowledge because it has been repeated and mutually confirmed rather than independently verified. This does not mean that every rumor must lose all informational content. Redundancy, correction, cross-checking, written records, and independent witnesses can reduce noise and sometimes restore lost information. Without such anchors, repeated verbal transmission becomes especially vulnerable to distortion. The classic rumor literature describes this through leveling, sharpening, and assimilation, whereby retellings lose some details, emphasize others, and reshape the story according to expectation, interest, or prior belief. [⇱](https://www.jstor.org/stable/2745703) A related pattern appears in serial reproduction experiments, where information passed from person to person shows greater loss of original material and greater distortion than information repeatedly reproduced by the same individual. [⇱](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S2211368114000485) A modern extension takes the same problem one step further by treating messages geometrically as embeddings. An embedding is a vector representation that places a word, sentence, or message in a high-dimensional space according to patterns of use and context. If two messages are used in similar contexts and carry similar meanings, their vectors tend to lie near one another. If retelling alters the context, emphasis, or implication of a message, its vector may move farther away. Diachronic word embeddings apply this idea across historical time, and _Hamilton_, _Leskovec_, and _Jurafsky_ use them to measure how word meanings change across corpora. [⇱](https://aclanthology.org/P16-1141.pdf) For whole messages rather than single words, current text-embedding research generalizes the same logic. Sentences, passages, and documents can be embedded as vectors and compared by similarity, although benchmarks such as MTEB show that performance depends strongly on the task rather than on one universally best embedding method. [⇱](https://huggingface.co/spaces/mteb/leaderboard) For gossip, this makes semantic drift measurable. Successive retellings **_M_****₀, ****_M_****₁, ****_M_****₂, …** can be represented as a path through semantic space, and distortion can be approximated by the distance between successive representations or by the divergence between distributions of message representations. This approach belongs more to distributional semantics, applied statistics, and computational linguistics than to classical _Shannon_ theory, but it preserves the same basic lesson. Content transmission requires correction mechanisms because circulation alone does not guarantee fidelity. The simulation below gives a schematic visualization of how gossip can drift through a semantic space. Each retelling **_Mₜ_** is represented by an embedding vector **_zₜ_****=****_E_****(****_Mₜ_****)** in a high-dimensional space, shown here through a two-dimensional projection **𝜋(****_zₜ_****)∈ℝ²**. The _black-circled point_ **_M_****₀** marks the source message. The _red trajectory_ shows an uncorrected chain of retellings, while the _blue trajectory_ shows a chain with periodic correction anchors, marked by _yellow stars_. These anchors can stand for written records, independent witnesses, source checks, or other stabilizing references. The _right panel_ plots the semantic distance from the source, approximated by: The norm is schematic, since a practical implementation may use cosine distance or another task-dependent similarity measure. The implicit model used here is a stochastic drift process in embedding space. Without correction, the vector **_zₜ_** moves under random transmission noise and persistent bias from emphasis, omission, assimilation, or moral framing. With correction, occasional anchoring steps pull the trajectory back toward **_z_****₀**, though not perfectly. Circulation alone does not guarantee semantic fidelity. > *An uncorrected rumor can remain active while its meaning drifts away from the source, whereas correction mechanisms can reduce that drift without eliminating noise entirely.* ### 4. Incentives and Gossip as a Strategic Game with Reputational Payoffs The previous section treated gossip as a noisy channel, where each retelling may preserve, distort, or reframe the original message. A further question is why people choose to transmit such messages in the first place. Gossip persists with such force because transmission can serve interests. It can inform, bond, warn, punish, entertain, raise status, or damage a rival. _Game-theoretic language_ becomes useful here because passing on a rumor can be treated as a decision with expected benefits and costs. _Foster's taxonomy of gossip functions_ identifies several motives that can be translated into utility terms, including information exchange, social bonding, status management, influence, and indirect aggression. [⇱](https://smg.media.mit.edu/library/Foster.gossipreview.pdf) _Baumeister_, _Zhang_, and _Vohs_ add the cultural-learning dimension by showing how gossip can turn norm violations and their consequences into memorable stories. [⇱](https://assets.csom.umn.edu/assets/71514.pdf) A minimal decision-theoretic representation treats transmission as a choice. For an agent **_i_**, passing rumor **_m_** to neighbor **_j_** has the _utility difference_: Here **_B_** collects the expected _benefits of transmission_, such as bonding with **_j_**, appearing informed, strengthening a coalition, warning others, or damaging a rival. The term **_C_** collects the _expected costs_, such as sanctions for deception, retaliation, loss of trust, or legal exposure. In the simplest version, **_i_** transmits **_m_** to **_j_** when: In a stochastic version, this threshold becomes a probability rule. A higher expected payoff makes transmission more likely rather than certain. where **𝜎** is an _increasing function_ and **𝜆>0** controls how strongly the payoff difference affects the transmission probability. If the payoffs are not normalized, **𝜆** has units of inverse utility so that the argument of **𝜎** remains dimensionless. This means that incentives explain not only why gossip exists but also how rapidly it moves through the network. Evolutionary models extend this incentive view by showing that gossip can be more than a private indulgence. _Pan_, _Hsiao_, _Nau_, and _Gelfand_ propose an agent-based evolutionary game-theoretic account in which _gossip proliferates because it disseminates reputational information and deters selfish behavior, thereby supporting cooperation_. [⇱](https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.2214160121) Their model explicitly combines reputation dissemination and selfishness deterrence, and their simulations show that gossipers can become dominant while cooperation remains high. This revises the usual moral caricature of gossip. Under certain conditions, gossip can serve as an enforcement mechanism for prosocial equilibria by making defection visible and costly. The same logic also suggests a strategic interpretation of the stifler compartment in the _Daley–Kendall_ tradition. A stifler need not be only someone who has lost interest in the rumor. In payoff terms, a stifler can be understood as an agent for whom further transmission has become unattractive across the available contacts, so that: for the relevant neighbors **_j_**. This may happen because the rumor has lost novelty, because the listener already knows it, or because further transmission now carries reputational risk. In both panels, _pale nodes_ are unaware, _red nodes_ are active spreaders, and _blue nodes_ are stiflers who no longer transmit the rumor. The _left panel_ shows a fixed-probability process, in which each active spreader transmits with the same baseline probability whenever contact occurs. The _right panel_ shows an incentive-aware process, in which the probability of transmission depends on the sender's payoff balance: For this visualization, **𝜆=1**, so the sensitivity factor is absorbed into **Δ****_U_**. The _lower-left chart_ compares the macroscopic cascades in the two models by plotting the fraction reached and the fraction still actively spreading. The _lower-right chart_ tracks the average payoff difference **⟨Δ****_U_****⟩** among active spreaders in the incentive-aware model. The key point is that contact structure alone does not determine rumor spread. In the fixed-probability model, transmission depends mainly on who meets whom. In the incentive-aware model, the same contact network is filtered through changing local incentives. Early in the process, novelty, status, warning value, or bonding can make transmission attractive, so the active-edge field is strong. As more people become informed, novelty falls, redundancy increases, and reputational risk can rise. The payoff balance declines, and spreaders become more likely to stop. The simulation therefore shows how microscopic payoff differences can alter local transmission probabilities and, through them, the shape and speed of the macroscopic cascade. ### 5. Platform Amplification and Gossip as a Self-Exciting Process The previous section treated transmission as a decision made by agents. On social platforms, however, that decision often does not occur in a neutral environment. Posts, reposts, comments, and likes are time-stamped, ranked, and redistributed, so each visible action can alter the conditions for later actions. Platform gossip therefore requires a dynamic model of amplification rather than only a network model of contact or a game-theoretic model of incentives. On social media, a rumor can accelerate through public engagements such as reposts, comments, and likes, which increase exposure and make later engagement more likely, rather than through person-to-person transmission alone. A standard mathematical model for this kind of reinforcement is the Hawkes process, a self-exciting point process in which each event temporarily raises the rate of future events. In one dimension, the intensity can be written as: Here **𝜆(****_t_****)** is the _instantaneous event rate_, **𝜇(****_t_****)** is the _baseline event rate_, and **𝜙** is an _excitation kernel_. Thus, **𝜆(****_t_****)**, **𝜇(****_t_****)**, and **𝜙(𝜏)** all have units of events per unit time, with **𝜙(𝜏)** representing the additional instantaneous rate generated at delay **𝜏** by one earlier event. In a social platform model, the events **_tᵢ_** may be posts, reshares, retweets, or comments. The kernel **𝜙** represents the fading influence of past activity. A recent repost can raise visibility and trigger further reposts, but its effect decays over time. This model captures the special feature that a platform rumor can unfold under feedback rather than pass purely along a fixed social graph. Visibility produces engagement, engagement produces more visibility, and the process can generate bursts even when the underlying interest is temporary. _Zhao_ and collaborators' SEISMIC model uses a self-exciting process to predict final retweet counts from early cascade signals. ⇱ Rizoiu and collaborators develop a related framework that separates exogenous promotion from endogenous response. [⇱](https://ssanner.github.io/papers/www17_hip.pdf) At this point, _gossip becomes algorithmic_. A ranking system can alter the baseline intensity **𝜇(****_t_****)** by exposing a rumor to new audiences, and it can also alter the effective excitation kernel by making each engagement more visible. In modern social media networks, the platform itself enters the dynamics that determine whether a rumor fades, circulates locally, or breaks into a burst of amplified attention. ### 6. Machine-Mediated Gossip The platform model becomes especially consequential once engagement and truth begin to diverge. _Vosoughi_, _Roy_, and _Aral_ analyzed large-scale Twitter rumor cascades and found that false news diffused farther, faster, more deeply, and more broadly than true news. They proposed novelty and emotional response as possible mechanisms. [⇱](https://www.science.org/doi/10.1126/science.aap9559) Their result does not mean that platforms automatically prefer falsehood, but it shows why systems organized around engagement may become vulnerable to content that is surprising, emotionally charged, sensational, or socially contagious. If engagement correlates more strongly with surprise than with truth, ranking systems can amplify the signals that help unreliable claims spread. _Germano_, _Gómez_, and _Sobbrio_ formalize this mechanism through an engagement-ranking model that can fuel misinformation and polarization under plausible assumptions. [⇱](https://bw.bse.eu/wp-content/uploads/2025/07/1501.pdf) Mathematically, _algorithmic curation_ can be treated schematically as an operator **𝒜** acting on the effective contact process. It changes which edges become active, how often they activate, and which audiences are exposed, thereby altering the parameters of the cascade rather than acting from outside it. [⇱](https://doi.org/10.1145/3614419.3644003) This also clarifies why the term "gossip" has acquired a second life in computer science. In distributed systems, gossip no longer means morally loaded talk about absent people. It means _randomized local exchange_. _Demers_ and collaborators described epidemic algorithms for replicated database maintenance, in which nodes spread updates through repeated contacts that resemble rumor propagation. [⇱](https://bitsavers.informatik.uni-stuttgart.de/pdf/xerox/parc/techReports/CSL-89-1_Epidemic_Algorithms_for_Replicated_Database_Maintenance.pdf) _Karp_, _Schindelhauer_, _Shenker_, and _Vöcking_ later analyzed push, pull, and push–pull rumor-spreading protocols, showing how quickly randomized local exchanges can disseminate information across a network. [⇱](https://archive.cone.informatik.uni-freiburg.de/pubs/rumor.pdf) The moral category disappears, while the algorithmic core remains because local exchange, partial knowledge, stochastic contact, and global dissemination are enough to produce system-wide propagation. In classical distributed systems, the word "gossip" refers mainly to dissemination. On platforms, ranking systems shape exposure, while gossip protocols show how information spreads through local exchanges once contact occurs. Human gossip shares this dissemination structure, but it also carries meaning, incentives, reputation, emotion, and risk. Recent machine-learning and AI systems add another level of complexity because they do more than transmit claims. They can infer, generate, summarize, mutate, verify, or hallucinate semantically structured content. [⇱](https://arxiv.org/abs/2311.05232) A language model that emits a plausible but false statement may seed a rumor. A large deployment may lower the cost of producing many such claims, while automated systems can raise the effective baseline intensity **𝜇(****_t_****)** by injecting content into many channels at once. At the same time, AI can support retrieval, ranking, correction, and fact-checking when it is embedded in controlled verification workflows. The same technology can act as an amplifier, a mutator, or a stabilizer, depending on how it is placed within the system. The empirical picture remains mixed. DeVerna and collaborators examine how LLM-generated fact-checks affect beliefs about political headlines and show that such interventions can also reduce discernment when the generated checks are inaccurate. [⇱](https://www.pnas.org/doi/10.1073/pnas.2322823121) In the opposite direction, Williams and collaborators show that LLMs can automate elements of election-disinformation operations, thereby lowering the cost of rumor production. [⇱](https://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371%2Fjournal.pone.0317421) A deeper risk arises when the system traps us in an epistemic feedback loop. Human text trains models, model output returns to human channels, and human channels may then re-ingest that output as apparent evidence. > *A modern theory of gossip therefore cannot treat content, transmission, and generation as separate processes.* In machine-mediated gossip, the distribution of content evolves under both human and artificial generators, while ranking systems reshape the rates at which different claims are viewed, repeated, corrected, or forgotten. ### Epilogue: When the Whisper Became a System Taken together, these models show why gossip cannot be reduced to a single mechanism. A rumor spreads through networks, changes through retelling, responds to incentives, bursts under platform feedback, and now circulates through machine systems that can rank, generate, mutate, and correct claims. Each layer alters the others. Network position affects exposure, exposure changes incentives, incentives shape transmission, transmission changes content, and content returns to the network as evidence, entertainment, warning, or myth. In this sense, what we call _virality_ is not a mystical phenomenon. It is a regime in which the effective reproduction of a claim exceeds the forces that slow, correct, or exhaust it. That regime may arise from topology, novelty, trust, emotional reward, ranking systems, or machine-generated repetition. A mathematical theory of gossip is therefore not a single equation but a framework for exploring how local exchanges, noisy content, strategic motives, and amplification mechanisms combine into large-scale patterns of social belief. At its best, a _mathematical theory of gossip_ helps explain which structural conditions make crowds manipulable and which design choices can reduce that manipulability without destroying legitimate communication. It clarifies why false stories can beat true stories in attention markets, and it can identify levers that alter the payoff structure, including norms, sanctions, friction, verification, and algorithmic design. The same equations that describe a rumor cascade may also help describe a correction cascade. The same network centralities that locate super-spreaders may also locate super-correctors. Human civilization has always depended on shared stories, but those stories now move through systems that can accelerate, rank, and reproduce them at unprecedented scale. > *As human communication changed, the whisper did not disappear but acquired servers, algorithms, rankings, and synthetic voices.* **_👏 If you enjoyed this article, please fifty-clap and repost it to your network._** > 🔭 If you find value in my article and would like to support my ongoing scientific research and publication projects, you can do so here: [https://ko-fi.com/samvaseghi](https://ko-fi.com/samvaseghi) #### References Allport, Gordon W., and Leo Postman. How Whispers Become Systems: The Mathematics of Gossip
How rumors move through networks, change through retelling, respond to incentives, and become amplified by platforms and AI
Amusing Gossip (1890) by Antonio Casanova y Estorach (1847–1896) (source).
Prologue: The Whisper That Shaped a Species
1. A Rumor on a Network and the Classical Daley–Kendall Model
2. Network Structure from Small Worlds to Heterogeneous Mean-Field Theory
3. Information Theory and Gossip as Noisy Transmission and Semantic Drift
4. Incentives and Gossip as a Strategic Game with Reputational Payoffs
5. Platform Amplification and Gossip as a Self-Exciting Process
Epilogue: When the Whisper Became a System
